lecture_title: Stanford CS231N | Spring 2025 | Lecture 12: Self-Supervised Learning

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The lecture begins with a brief review of the previous lecture on GPUs and large-scale training, transitioning into the main topic: Self-Supervised Learning. The instructor highlights the primary challenge in scaling up neural networks: the massive requirement for manually labeled data. Self-supervised learning is introduced as a paradigm to bypass manual labeling by defining a 'pretext task' on large volumes of unlabeled data. A network (encoder) is trained on this pretext task, and the learned representations are then transferred to a 'downstream task' (the actual application of interest, like classification or segmentation) which typically has a much smaller, labeled dataset. The labels for the pretext task are generated automatically from the raw data.
key_takeaways: Scaling neural networks is traditionally bottlenecked by the need for massive manually labeled datasets.
Self-supervised learning solves this by using a pretext task on unlabeled data to learn robust feature representations.
A trained encoder from a pretext task can be fine-tuned or linearly probed on a downstream task with limited labeled data.

table_of_contents.topic_title: Pretext Tasks: Image Transformations and Evaluation Metrics
table_of_contents.detailed_summary: This section outlines how pretext tasks are defined using image transformations. Examples include image completion, rotation prediction, jigsaw puzzles, and colorization. The core hypothesis is that solving these tasks requires the model to develop 'visual common sense' and learn high-quality features. The instructor also details how to evaluate self-supervised models. Key evaluation methods include: measuring pretext task performance, assessing representation quality (via linear evaluation protocols, clustering, and t-SNE visualizations), testing robustness/generalization, and evaluating computational efficiency. The most critical metric is the performance on downstream tasks using either linear probing (freezing the encoder and training a final linear layer) or full fine-tuning (updating all model weights).
key_takeaways: Pretext tasks force the model to learn meaningful visual structures from images without human labels.
Linear probing tests the quality of frozen representations, while fine-tuning adapts the entire model to a new task.
Downstream task performance is the ultimate benchmark for a self-supervised learning algorithm.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The instructor dives into the specific pretext task of predicting image rotations. The model is fed an image rotated by either 0, 90, 180, or 270 degrees and must classify which of the four rotations was applied. To succeed, the model must implicitly learn the canonical orientation of objects, requiring a holistic understanding of visual semantics. Results demonstrate that a network pre-trained on this simple 4-way classification task yields feature extractors that perform highly on complex downstream tasks (like Pascal VOC classification, detection, and segmentation), drastically outperforming randomly initialized networks and approaching the performance of models pre-trained on supervised ImageNet labels. Visualization of attention maps reveals that self-supervised models often look at the entire object rather than just discriminative parts.
key_takeaways: Rotation prediction is a simple 4-way classification task that generates highly effective feature representations.
Self-supervised models tend to learn more holistic attention maps compared to supervised models, which focus narrowly on discriminative features.

table_of_contents.topic_title: Pretext Task 2: Relative Patch Location & Jigsaw Puzzles
table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Another popular pretext task involves understanding the spatial layout of an image. One approach takes a 3x3 grid of image patches and asks the network to predict the position of an outer patch relative to the center patch (an 8-way classification task). An evolution of this is the 'Jigsaw Puzzle' task, where all 9 patches are shuffled, and the model must output the correct permutation. Since 9! (over 300,000) permutations is computationally impractical, researchers select a subset of 64 plausible permutations with the maximum distance between them. The task then becomes a 64-way classification problem, forcing the model to learn edge continuity and part-to-whole relationships.
key_takeaways: Spatial layout tasks force the model to understand how different parts of an object relate to one another.
To make the jigsaw puzzle task tractable, the label space is reduced from 9! to 64 maximally distant permutations.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The lecture covers image inpainting as a pretext task using Context Encoders. In this setup, a central block of an image is masked out, and an autoencoder architecture (an encoder followed by a decoder) is trained to reconstruct the missing pixels based on the surrounding context. The loss is computed solely on the masked region using Mean Squared Error (MSE). Because MSE alone tends to produce blurry, averaged outputs, the authors introduced an additional adversarial loss (conceptually similar to GANs) to ensure the reconstructed patches look realistic and sharp. This task forces the encoder to capture deep semantic representations of the image.
key_takeaways: Image inpainting requires the network to infer missing content from surrounding context.
Reconstruction loss (MSE) causes blurriness, which can be mitigated by adding an adversarial loss.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Image colorization separates an image into lightness (L channel) and color (AB channels) in the LAB color space. The pretext task is to predict the AB channels given the L channel. An extension of this is the 'Split-Brain Autoencoder', which utilizes two separate networks: one predicts color from lightness, and the other predicts lightness from color. This cross-channel prediction can also be applied to RGB-Depth data. The concept is extended to video colorization, where the model uses a colored reference frame to colorize subsequent grayscale frames. To succeed, the model calculates attention between target pixels and reference pixels, implicitly learning to track objects and segments across time without any manual tracking labels.
key_takeaways: Colorization leverages the natural separation of illumination and color in the LAB space.
Video colorization forces the model to learn temporal coherence, naturally leading to emergent object tracking and keypoint detection without labels.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The instructor introduces Masked Autoencoders (MAE), a highly influential 2021 framework based on Vision Transformers (ViTs). MAE works by dividing an image into non-overlapping patches and heavily masking them (typically around a 75% masking ratio). The encoder only processes the unmasked (visible) patches, making it highly computationally efficient, allowing for massive encoder sizes. A lightweight decoder then takes the encoder's output embeddings along with shared, trainable 'mask tokens' to reconstruct the original image. The loss (MSE) is computed only on the masked patches. MAE's asymmetrical design and high masking ratio force the model to learn deep semantic representations rather than just local pixel correlations.
key_takeaways: MAE uses an aggressive 75% masking ratio, making the reconstruction task challenging and semantically meaningful.
The encoder is highly efficient because it only processes the 25% visible patches, allowing the architecture to scale massively.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: A limitation of transformation-based pretext tasks is that the learned features might be too tailored to the specific task rather than generalizing well. Contrastive learning offers a more general approach: representations of the same object (different augmented views) are pulled together ('attracted' as positive pairs), while representations of different objects are pushed apart ('repelled' as negative pairs). The instructor introduces the InfoNCE (Information Noise Contrastive Estimation) loss, which mathematically resembles a cross-entropy loss for an N-way softmax classifier. Minimizing the InfoNCE loss maximizes a lower bound on the mutual information between positive pairs. Crucially, the tightness of this theoretical bound relies on having a very large number of negative samples (N).
key_takeaways: Contrastive learning aims to make representations invariant to augmentations of the same image while distancing them from other images.
The InfoNCE loss maximizes mutual information between positive pairs.
A large number of negative samples is required for contrastive learning to be highly effective.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: SimCLR is presented as a straightforward framework for contrastive learning. It takes a batch of images, applies two different random augmentations (like cropping, color distortion, and blur) to each, and passes them through an encoder. Instead of computing contrastive loss directly on the representation 'h', SimCLR introduces a non-linear projection head (MLP) to map 'h' to a lower-dimensional space 'z'. The contrastive loss (cosine similarity) is calculated in the 'z' space. This design choice prevents the main representation 'h' from discarding useful information that might be destroyed by the contrastive objective. Because SimCLR relies on the batch itself for negative samples, it requires massive batch sizes (e.g., 8192) to perform well, necessitating heavy TPU compute.
key_takeaways: SimCLR heavily relies on strong data augmentation (crop, color distort) to create positive pairs.
A non-linear projection head protects the core representation space from losing information irrelevant to the contrastive task.
SimCLR's major bottleneck is the requirement for massive batch sizes to source enough negative samples.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: MoCo addresses the massive batch size limitation of SimCLR. Instead of relying solely on the current mini-batch for negative samples, MoCo maintains a running queue (dictionary) of negative keys from previous batches. Because backpropagation through a long queue is impossible, MoCo decouples the encoders. A 'Query' encoder is updated via standard backpropagation, while a 'Key' (Momentum) encoder is updated slowly using an exponential moving average of the Query encoder's weights. This allows MoCo to maintain a massive negative sample dictionary without requiring huge GPU/TPU memory. A follow-up, MoCo v2, integrated SimCLR's projection head and strong data augmentations, resulting in superior performance with manageable batch sizes.
key_takeaways: MoCo decouples dictionary size from batch size by using a running queue of negative samples.
To ensure feature consistency in the queue without backpropagation, a slow-moving Momentum Encoder is utilized.
MoCo v2 combines the architectural efficiency of MoCo with the projection head and augmentations of SimCLR.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The lecture concludes by touching on alternative self-supervised frameworks. Contrastive Predictive Coding (CPC) applies contrastive learning sequentially, using past context to predict future tokens (used heavily in audio and visual sequences). DINO (Self-Distillation with No Labels) is briefly mentioned as a powerful Vision Transformer-based framework. Unlike SimCLR or MoCo, DINO operates without negative samples. It relies on a teacher-student architecture where the student predicts the output of a momentum-updated teacher. DINO is notable because its self-attention maps naturally segment objects in images entirely unsupervised.
key_takeaways: CPC extends contrastive learning to sequential data, predicting future states to learn context.
DINO achieves state-of-the-art self-supervised representations without using negative samples, yielding emergent object segmentation properties.
